segments.text:
Genome Sequencing has novel applications including:
Sampling species diversity in the environment
Determination of the minimal subset of genes necessary for life
Laboratory Synthesis of Genomes/Genome swap

Major points:
Many bacterial species cannot be cultivated in the lab
1 billion bases of non-redundant sequence was generated
1800 genomes sampled including 148 new ones
1.2 million new genes

Minimal Genome at each Metabolic level
Photoautotrophs
able to grow in a medium of only CO2, light, and 	inorganic salts
Heterotrophs
organic carbon and energy source like glucose, and 	inorganic salts
Fastidious heterotrophs
undefined components
Obligate intracellular parasites
Organelles

Mycoplasma genitalium	Haemophilus influenzae
Gram positive				Gram negative
469 proteins				1703 proteins
240 genes overlap
+
22 genes to fill in the gaps for essential         		                biochemical pathways
-
6 genes for functionally redundant and host cell specific
256 essential genes
Starting with just the DNA sequence, one can make a functional viral genome

In vitro Synthesis of Polio Genome
DNA synthesizer makes oligos
69 bp average length
+ and – polarity
have overlapping complementary sequences at their termini
Annealed into make 400-600 bp segments
Segments ligated into vectors to make fragments

In vitro Synthesis of Polio Genome
(Continued)

Synthesized RNA
Added it to Hela Cell extract
(free of nuclei)
Extract made virions
Virions used to infect transgenic mice    	 	 (have human CD155 receptor)
Caused paralysis or death
Protein expression pattern of transplant matches donor, not recipient

Project goals:
Maintain “wild-type” phenotype
Minimize genome instability (get rid of repeats)
All tRNAs moved to a “neochromosome”
Get rid of introns
Remove restriction sites to facilitate assembly
Put in Lox sites (5000) in non-essential genes

Important Points
Genome Sequencing
Random
Minimal genomes
Synthetic
Viral
Bacterial
Eukaryotic
Genome switching
